snippet.description:
Welcome to the Ultimate Flagship Battery Drain Test (2026) — where hype meets reality.
We lined up 12 flagship smartphones, including the iPhone 17 Pro Max, Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra, iQOO 13, OnePlus 13, and more — to find out which phone truly delivers the best battery endurance under real-world conditions. 🔋⚡

Every phone ran the same setup — 120 Hz refresh rate, full brightness, Wi-Fi ON, and 23 °C ambient temperature. From YouTube playback to 4 K video recording, we pushed each phone to its thermal and endurance limits.

 I’ve been tired of phones feeling too heavy for a while. Reviewing the iPhone Air recently felt like a breath of fresh air. Using a phone that is extremely light and easy to use in one hand was a great experience, and you can watch our video all about it linked below. It was immediately after finishing this review that I switched to iPhone 17 Pro for testing, and to cut to the chase, I haven’t liked it very much. 
 When I first saw the design reveal for the 17 Pro, I immediately thought the design wasn’t for me. The separation between the two sections on the back just doesn’t look right to me, and feels like a sharp departure from Apple’s usually more aesthetically pleasing designs. This, along with the phone’s significant thickness makes for a design that reminds me of the way iPhones with lifeproof cases looked around 2010. I understood why people did this back then to protect their phones, just like I understand why Apple opted to design this phone in a such a way to allow for features like the vapor chamber and increased battery capacity. Design however, is important to me, and the design of this phone strikes me as just plain ugly.
 Despite having a very minor increase in weight over the 16 Pro (the phone I’ve dailyed over the last year) this phone feels significantly heavier. I assume this has something to do with weight distribution and the slight increase in thickness, but its a difference that I fully perceive. The phone is kind of a brick, and multiple people I showed it to agreed that it was unusually heavy. I know you can argue that this shouldn’t matter since the battery is bigger, but to me it does. I’ve said before, I’m not a heavy user, so the admittedly better battery when compared to the Air doesn’t mean much to me. I went out of my way a few times with this phone to try to use it a lot more than I usually would, and I admit, the battery is pretty impressive but it just doesn’t really matter that much to me. I’d personally prefer a thinner and lighter phone like the Air given that both the Pro and the Air easily get me through a fully day. I just really enjoy using my phone in one hand and/or holding it to watch videos, both tasks that feel uncomfortable given the phone’s weight. I suppose this is what it means for a phone to be “Pro” in Apple’s lineup, in the same way their MacBook Pros are quite thick (though the iPad Pro is thinner than the iPad Air). If you’re someone who does “Pro” things on your phone like create and edit content all day, I’m sure the extra battery will be much appreciated. For the people however who simply buy the Pro iPhone because its the “premium” model however, it’s unclear if this is the phone to get now that the Air Exists.
 The 3 camera set up is undeniably more full featured than the Air’s 1 camera, with the increased quality of the telephoto camera coming as a welcome addition from my 16 Pro. As I mentioned in my iPhone Air Review however, I rarely use the ultra wide or telephoto cameras on my phone, and would gladly give up the two extra cameras for a thinner and lighter phone.
 An area where I admit I was very impressed with this phone after just reviewing the Air is the speaker. The speaker on this phone is great, and sounds quite a bit better than the one on my 16 Pro (compared to the Air its night and day). This is perhaps the one area where the heavier design leads to a feature I meaningfully appreciate.
 Its possible a few years ago I would have been completely blown away by the 17 Pro and dying to upgrade to it. After all, its hard to argue that this is not the most fully featured, powerful iPhone to date. With that said, my time with the iPhone Air changed me and made me realize what I truly value in a phone. This also probably has something to do with owning an iPad Pro, my primary device used for both computing and content consumption. The point of a phone at this point for me is that its a portable and easy to use communication/commuting device. In a world where the iPhone Air exists, the 17 Pro just misses the mark for me.

Apple's new iPhone 17 Pro Max comes with an incredible 8x optical telephoto lens, equivalent to roughly 200mm. But how does it compare to the Sony RX100 VII, which has a 24-200mm optical zoom range and a much larger 1-inch sensor?

This isn't just about specs. We're putting them head-to-head in a series of real-world tests to see if the iPhone 17 Pro has finally closed the gap, or if the RX100 VII still wins the zoom war.

snippet.title: Fix Slow Keyboard Typing after iOS 26 Update (ANY iPhone) - 17 (Pro, Max), 16, 15, 14, 13
snippet.description:
Fix your iPhone keyboard lagging or typing slow after installing iOS 26? In this video, I’ll show you quick and easy fixes to speed up your keyboard instantly. From resetting the keyboard dictionary to turning off Slow Keys and rebooting your iPhone, these steps work on all iPhones including iPhone 17, 17 Pro, and 17 Pro Max. Watch till the end to get your smooth typing back in seconds!

00:00 intro
00:08 Solution 1
00:44 Solution 2
01:02 Solution 3
01:22 Change Keyboard


#iPhone #iOS26 #KeyboardLag #iPhone17


Here’s a quick fix for your iPhone’s keyboard lag or slowness while typing on the iOS 26 update on any iPhone.

The first solution is to reset the keyboard dictionary. The iPhone keyboard dictionary learns and saves the words you type often to improve autocorrect and suggestions while adapting to your personal typing style.

Go to the Settings app, scroll to General, then scroll down to the Transfer or Reset iPhone option. Tap on Reset, and then choose Reset Keyboard Dictionary.

Enter your iPhone lock screen passcode and verify it to reset the dictionary.

That’s it. Check your iPhone and reboot once. Hope this fixes your problem.

Still not working? Then move to the next solution. Turn off the Slow Keys option if it’s on, and then force restart your iPhone.

Go to the Settings app, scroll to Accessibility, and under Keyboard and Typing, tap on Slow Keys and turn off the toggle.

That’s it. Now hard reboot to apply the change by quickly pressing and releasing the volume up button, then the volume down button, and finally, press and hold the power button until you see the Apple logo on the screen.

Enter your lock screen passcode and check if the issue’s fixed.

Another important thing to check is to use Apple’s default keyboard. Don’t use a third-party keyboard that’s not compatible with the app you’re using on your iPhone.

Hold the globe icon key and switch to Apple’s original keyboard, or delete any extra keyboard you installed under Keyboard settings.

Swipe left to delete the keyboard from your iPhone.

I hope you found this solution helpful. Don’t forget to keep your iPhone and apps updated to the latest version to fix minor bugs and compatibility issues.

Also, make sure your iPhone’s updated to the latest iOS version.
